meta.name: French Country Butter Mashed Potatoes
meta.subtitle: Pomme purée-style mash: buttery, silky, and richly seasoned.

source.name: ssa.cuisines.french_country
notes: Canonical SSA recipe modeled after French-style pomme purée: steam-dried potatoes, warm dairy, lots of butter, finished silky.
Designed for predictable results, batch prep, and service holding guidance.

summary: Rich French country mashed potatoes: Yukon Golds cooked until tender, steam-dried, then mashed with warm milk/cream and plenty of butter until silky. Seasoned simply with salt and white pepper.

yield.notes: Serves 6 as a side. Scale linearly.

notes: Best texture same-day; leftovers reheat well with added dairy.
Freezing can make texture slightly grainy unless re-whipped with hot dairy.
Butter-forward, creamy, and cleanly seasoned.
French style favors smooth texture: ricer/food mill recommended.

optional.label: Food mill
optional.notes: Excellent for ultra-smooth mash; use medium disk.

optional.label: Stand mixer
optional.notes: Use cautiously on low speed only; overmixing can make potatoes gummy.

notes: Contains dairy (butter, milk/cream). Use plant butter and oat milk for dairy-free variant.

ingredients.label: Yukon Gold potatoes, peeled and cut into 2-inch chunks
notes: Yukon Gold gives a naturally buttery flavor and creamy texture.
Russets work too (lighter/fluffier); reduce mixing and add dairy gradually.

ingredients.label: Kosher salt (for cooking water and seasoning)
notes: Salt the cooking water well—this seasons the potatoes from within.

ingredients.label: Unsalted butter, cubed
notes: For very rich French-style purée, increase to 10–12 tbsp.
Keep butter cold until ready to incorporate (then melt into hot potatoes).

ingredients.label: Whole milk (or half-and-half)
notes: Warm the milk before adding to avoid cooling the potatoes.

ingredients.label: White pepper (or black pepper), to taste
notes: White pepper keeps the mash visually clean; black pepper is fine.

ingredients.label: Ground nutmeg (tiny pinch, optional)
notes: Use a very small pinch—classic French accent.

steps.title: Prep potatoes and start in cold salted water
instructions: Peel potatoes and cut into evenly sized 2-inch chunks for even cooking.
Place potatoes in a large pot and cover with cold water by 1–2 inches.
Add about 2 tsp kosher salt to the water.
checks: Potatoes are fully submerged in cold water.

steps.title: Boil gently until very tender
instructions: Bring to a boil over high heat, then reduce to a steady simmer.
Cook 15–20 minutes until a knife slides in with almost no resistance.
Do not undercook—slightly over-tender is better for smooth mash.
checks: Potatoes are fully tender; centers are not chalky.

steps.title: Warm the milk (and optional cream)
instructions: While potatoes cook, warm milk (and optional cream) in a small saucepan over low heat.
Heat until steaming but not boiling; keep warm.
checks: Milk/cream is hot to the touch but not scalded.

steps.title: Drain and steam-dry the potatoes
instructions: Drain potatoes thoroughly in a colander.
Return potatoes to the hot pot over low heat for 1–2 minutes, shaking/stirring gently.
This drives off excess water for a richer, less watery mash.
checks: Potatoes look dry and slightly chalky on the surface (not wet).

steps.title: Rice (preferred) or mash the potatoes
instructions: For the smoothest result, pass hot potatoes through a ricer or food mill into a warm bowl.
If using a masher, mash gently while potatoes are hot; avoid aggressive mixing.
checks: Potatoes are broken down evenly with no large lumps.

steps.title: Incorporate butter, then add warm dairy gradually
instructions: Add butter to the hot potatoes and fold until melted and absorbed.
Add warm milk (and cream if using) a little at a time, folding or stirring until silky.
Stop when texture is creamy and holds soft peaks; do not overwork.
checks: Mash is glossy and smooth, not gluey.

steps.title: Season, adjust consistency, and serve
instructions: Taste and adjust salt. Add white pepper (and optional nutmeg).
If too thick, add a splash more warm milk. If too loose, cook 1–2 minutes over low heat, stirring gently.
Serve warm; garnish with chives if desired.
checks: Seasoning is balanced and texture is creamy.

notes: Perfect with roast chicken, beef stew, or saucy mains.
For a classic French plate, pair with a simple green salad and vinaigrette.
Cool quickly and refrigerate within 2 hours.
Mash firms up in the fridge; reheat with added warm milk/cream.
Freezing is possible but texture may change slightly.
For best results, reheat gently and re-whip with hot dairy and a little butter.
Stovetop: reheat over low heat with splashes of milk, stirring gently.
Microwave: heat in short bursts, stirring between, adding milk as needed.
To hold for service: keep covered in a warm oven (~200°F / 95°C) or bain-marie, stirring occasionally.
Add a pat of butter on top to prevent drying.

core: Do not leave at room temperature longer than 2 hours (1 hour if very warm room).
Reheat leftovers until steaming hot throughout.

notes: For the silkiest texture, avoid food processors (they can make potatoes gummy).
Add butter before milk—fat coats starch and improves texture.
Warm dairy is key: cold milk cools the potatoes and can cause gumminess.
Increase butter to 10–12 tbsp and use half milk + half cream.
Pass through a fine sieve after ricing for ultra-silky texture (optional).
Simmer 3–6 peeled garlic cloves with the potatoes, then mash together.
Or fold in roasted garlic paste at the end.
Use plant butter and warm oat milk (or unsweetened almond milk).
Add olive oil for richness; season a touch more aggressively.

remixIdeas: Crisp potato cakes in a skillet
Thicken soups/stews by stirring in a spoonful
